American Headache Society Annual Meeting By Richard Gawel Fact checked by Shenaz Bagha Purpose-built for physicians Ask Learn more Read more Migraines do not improve, but health care utilization decreases following GLP-1 initiation June 19, 2026 3 min read By Richard Gawel Fact checked by Shenaz Bagha Add topic to email alerts Please provide your email address to receive an email when new articles are posted on Headache . Subscribe Added to email alerts You've successfully added Headache to your alerts. You will receive an email when new content is published. Click Here to Manage Email Alerts You've successfully added Headache to your alerts. You will receive an email when new content is published. Click Here to Manage Email Alerts Back to Healio We were unable to process your request. Please try again later. If you continue to have this issue please contact customerservice@slackinc.com . Back to Healio Save Share LinkedIn Facebook X (Twitter) Bluesky Threads Email Copy Link Key takeaways: Patients lost weight with GLP-1 receptor agonist use but migraine symptoms persisted. Compared with topiramate, patients who used GLP-1s experienced less treatment escalation. Patients with migraine who began using GLP-1 receptor agonists for obesity did not experience any significant changes in headache outcomes, according to data presented at the American Headache Society 68th Annual Scientific Meeting. However, initiation of these drugs was associated with less health care utilization and treatment escalation compared with topiramate, Scott Yuan, MD, PhD, director, Jefferson Headache Center, Thomas Jefferson University, and colleagues wrote. Data derived from Acar V, et al. Real-world outcomes of GLP-1 receptor agonist initiation in migraine: A propensity-matched comparison with topiramate. Presented at: American Headache Society Congress; June 4-7, 2026; Orlando. “GLP-1 receptor agonists have growing biologic plausibility in migraine , but clinical evidence remains limited, inconsistent, and largely derived from small or highly selected populations,” Yuan told Healio. ‘We conducted two complementary real-world analyses to explore this question, recognizing that the goal was to generate hypotheses rather than establish efficacy.”. Scott Yuan In the first analysis, Yuan and colleagues conducted a retrospective longitudinal cohort study that included 178 patients (mean age, 46.6 years; 91.9% female; baseline BMI, 37.2 kg/m 2 ) taking GLP-1 RAs, predominantly semaglutide (Ozempic/Wegovy, Novo Nordisk). A total of 85.3% had a chronic migraine diagnosis. Obesity can drive migraine, suggesting that weight loss may benefit these patients. At 12 months, the researchers continued, the cohort experienced significant decreases in BMI (beta = –0.34; 95% CI, –0.43 to –0.25), according to the researchers. However, longitudinal changes in monthly headache days (beta = 0.045), days with moderate to severe headache (–0.026), average pain intensity (beta = –0.022), worst pain intensity (beta = 0.005) and Migraine Disability Assessment (beta = 1.799) scores were not significant. Analysis of 41 patients with previous secondary diagnoses of headache yielded similar non-significant findings, the researchers wrote. The second analysis was a retrospective study of patients with migraine who initiated GLP-1 RAs (n = 38,876; age at index, 47.6 years; 87% female; BMI = 36.4 kg/m 2 ) or topiramate (n = 38,876; age at index, 48 years; 87.6% female; BMI = 36 kg/m 2 ). Risk ratios for less health care utilization from index through 365 days with GLP-1 RA initiation compared with topiramate included 0.833 (95% CI, 0.811-0.855) for ED visits, 0.87 (95% CI, 0.844-0.896) for hospitalizations and 0.745 (95% CI, 0.682-0.814) for nerve block procedures. Similarly, risk ratios for treatment escalation included 0.8 (95% CI, 0.776-0.576) for triptan use, 0.76 (95% CI, 0.728-0.793) for parenteral therapies, 0.534 (95% CI, 0.495-0.819) for antidepressants, 0.292 (95% CI, 0.243-0.352) for valproate, 0.539 (95% CI, 0.5-0.58) for gepants and 0.366 (95% CI, 0.336-0.4) for calcitonin gene-related peptide monoclonal antibodies. “The two studies produced conflicting signals. A single-center cohort showed no meaningful improvement in headache outcomes despite weight loss, while a large EHR analysis suggested reduced health care utilization,” Yuan said. “Together, these findings highlight the uncertainty and limitations of current real-world data rather than providing a definitive answer.” Yuan and colleagues called for additional studies with larger samples and follow-up beyond 12 months to determine the impact of GLP-1 RAs on patients with migraine. Yuan also noted that these indirect and observational findings should be considered hypothesis-generating and that clinicians should not based any changes in practice on them. “Given important limitations, including confounding, limited treatment exposure data and population bias, there is insufficient evidence to conclude whether GLP-1RAs meaningfully improve migraine,” Yuan told Healio. “ Prospective, controlled studies with direct headache outcomes and better measurement of treatment exposure are needed to determine whether GLP-1RAs have a causal role in migraine. Key takeaways:
Patients lost weight with GLP-1 receptor agonist use but migraine symptoms persisted. Compared with topiramate, patients who used GLP-1s experienced less treatment escalation.
Patients lost weight with GLP-1 receptor agonist use but migraine symptoms persisted.
Compared with topiramate, patients who used GLP-1s experienced less treatment escalation.
Patients with migraine who began using GLP-1 receptor agonists for obesity did not experience any significant changes in headache outcomes, according to data presented at the American Headache Society 68th Annual Scientific Meeting.
However, initiation of these drugs was associated with less health care utilization and treatment escalation compared with topiramate, Scott Yuan, MD, PhD, director, Jefferson Headache Center, Thomas Jefferson University, and colleagues wrote.
Scott Yuan, MD, PhD,
Data derived from Acar V, et al. Real-world outcomes of GLP-1 receptor agonist initiation in migraine: A propensity-matched comparison with topiramate. Presented at: American Headache Society Congress; June 4-7, 2026; Orlando.
“GLP-1 receptor agonists have growing biologic plausibility in migraine , but clinical evidence remains limited, inconsistent, and largely derived from small or highly selected populations,” Yuan told Healio.
biologic plausibility in migraine
‘We conducted two complementary real-world analyses to explore this question, recognizing that the goal was to generate hypotheses rather than establish efficacy.”.
Scott Yuan
In the first analysis, Yuan and colleagues conducted a retrospective longitudinal cohort study that included 178 patients (mean age, 46.6 years; 91.9% female; baseline BMI, 37.2 kg/m 2 ) taking GLP-1 RAs, predominantly semaglutide (Ozempic/Wegovy, Novo Nordisk). A total of 85.3% had a chronic migraine diagnosis.
2
Obesity can drive migraine, suggesting that weight loss may benefit these patients. At 12 months, the researchers continued, the cohort experienced significant decreases in BMI (beta = –0.34; 95% CI, –0.43 to –0.25), according to the researchers.
However, longitudinal changes in monthly headache days (beta = 0.045), days with moderate to severe headache (–0.026), average pain intensity (beta = –0.022), worst pain intensity (beta = 0.005) and Migraine Disability Assessment (beta = 1.799) scores were not significant.
Analysis of 41 patients with previous secondary diagnoses of headache yielded similar non-significant findings, the researchers wrote.
The second analysis was a retrospective study of patients with migraine who initiated GLP-1 RAs (n = 38,876; age at index, 47.6 years; 87% female; BMI = 36.4 kg/m 2 ) or topiramate (n = 38,876; age at index, 48 years; 87.6% female; BMI = 36 kg/m 2 ).
or topiramate
Risk ratios for less health care utilization from index through 365 days with GLP-1 RA initiation compared with topiramate included 0.833 (95% CI, 0.811-0.855) for ED visits, 0.87 (95% CI, 0.844-0.896) for hospitalizations and 0.745 (95% CI, 0.682-0.814) for nerve block procedures.
Similarly, risk ratios for treatment escalation included 0.8 (95% CI, 0.776-0.576) for triptan use, 0.76 (95% CI, 0.728-0.793) for parenteral therapies, 0.534 (95% CI, 0.495-0.819) for antidepressants, 0.292 (95% CI, 0.243-0.352) for valproate, 0.539 (95% CI, 0.5-0.58) for gepants and 0.366 (95% CI, 0.336-0.4) for calcitonin gene-related peptide monoclonal antibodies.
“The two studies produced conflicting signals. A single-center cohort showed no meaningful improvement in headache outcomes despite weight loss, while a large EHR analysis suggested reduced health care utilization,” Yuan said.
“Together, these findings highlight the uncertainty and limitations of current real-world data rather than providing a definitive answer.”
Yuan and colleagues called for additional studies with larger samples and follow-up beyond 12 months to determine the impact of GLP-1 RAs on patients with migraine. Yuan also noted that these indirect and observational findings should be considered hypothesis-generating and that clinicians should not based any changes in practice on them.
“Given important limitations, including confounding, limited treatment exposure data and population bias, there is insufficient evidence to conclude whether GLP-1RAs meaningfully improve migraine,” Yuan told Healio.
“ Prospective, controlled studies with direct headache outcomes and better measurement of treatment exposure are needed to determine whether GLP-1RAs have a causal role in migraine. Future work should also focus on identifying specific patient subgroups and addressing key confounders to clarify these conflicting signals.”
“
For more information:
Scott Yuan, MD, PhD, can be reached at neurology@healio.com .

articles.title: Cognitive symptoms improve in migraine with eptinezumab
articles.text:
Close Ask Healio AI New!

American Headache Society Annual Meeting By Richard Gawel Fact checked by Richard Gawel Purpose-built for physicians Ask Learn more Read more Cognitive symptoms improve in migraine with eptinezumab June 18, 2026 3 min read By Richard Gawel Fact checked by Richard Gawel Add topic to email alerts Please provide your email address to receive an email when new articles are posted on Headache . Back to Healio Save Share LinkedIn Facebook X (Twitter) Bluesky Threads Email Copy Link Key takeaways: Patients with migraine report bothersome cognitive symptoms. Eptinezumab targets patients whose previous therapies have failed. Brain fog was the most common cognitive symptom. Adults with migraine whose previous preventive therapy had failed saw improvements in cognitive symptoms with 12 months of eptinezumab, according to a poster presented at the American Headache Society 68th Annual Scientific Meeting. Eptinezumab (Vypeti; Lundbeck) targets calcitonin gene-related peptides (CGRPs) to prevent episodic and chronic migraine, according to Richard B. Lipton, MD, Edwin S. Lowe Chair in Neurology at Albert Einstein College of Medicine, and colleagues. Data derived from Starling AJ, et al. Real-world effectiveness of eptinezumab treatment on migraine-related cognitive symptoms in participants in whom 1 prior CGRP-targeted therapy had failed: 12-month results from the INFUSE study. Presented at: American Headache Society 68 th Annual Scientific Meeting; June 4-7, 2026; Orlando. “One of the most exciting things that has happened in migraine ever is the advent of CGRP-targeted therapies,” Lipton told Healio. Among four monoclonal antibodies and two small molecules that target CGRPs to prevent migraine approved by the FDA, eptinezumab (Vyepti, Lundbeck) is the only intravenously administered treatment, the researchers wrote. “In that context, understanding the real-world effectiveness of eptinezumab in people who have tried and failed other CGRP-targeting therapies is very important,” Lipton said. The prospective, non-interventional, real-world INFUSE effectiveness study began with 190 adults (88.9% women), including 38 (20%) aged 18 to 34 years, 102 (53.7%) aged 35 to 54 years and 45 (23.7%) aged 55 to 74 years. A total of 154 patients (81.1%) had been diagnosed with chronic migraine , with 47 (24.7%) reporting one failed CGRP-targeted preventive therapy, 38 (20%) reporting two failed therapies and 105 (55.3%) reporting three or more failed therapies . “If somebody started on a migraine preventive and they discontinued it, they discontinued for two main reasons,” Lipton said. “One is it either doesn’t work well enough, and so they’re dissatisfied with efficacy, or the medication causes side effects.” Lipton attributed these different outcomes to migraine’s biological heterogeneity. “People may respond well to different mechanisms or even different treatments that target a similar mechanism,” he said. “Migraine is really not a one-size-fits-all condition.” The prevalence of cognitive impairments at baseline included 178 patients (93.7%) with brain fog, 150 (78.9%) with difficulty in making decisions, 157 (82.6%) with difficulty in reading comprehension and 157 (82.6%) with difficulty in complex tasks. Percentages of patients reporting that these symptoms were “moderately” to “very” or “extremely” bothersome included 82.1% for brain fog, 64.7% for difficulty in making decisions, 60% for difficulty with reading comprehension and 62.6% for difficulty with complex tasks. “The cognitive burden of migraine is very substantial,” Lipton said. Treatment included 100 mg or 300 mg of eptinezumab every 12 weeks. Among 91 patients with data on brain fog at 12 months, 27.5% said it had “minimally” improved and 37.4% said it had “much” or “very much” improved. With 80 patients reporting 12-month data on difficulty in making decisions, 21.3% said it had minimally improved and 40% said it had “much” or “very much” improved. The 84 patients with 12-month data on difficulty with reading comprehension included 23.8% who reported they had minimally improved and 35.7% who had “much” or “very much” improved. Among 82 with 12-month data on difficulty with complex tasks, 20.7% said it had “minimally” improved and 39% said it had “much” or “very much” improved. Although the INFUSE study did not collect safety data, Lipton said, data reported through established channels indicated a positive safety profile as well. Considering the cognitive burden experienced by these patients at baseline, the researchers said these findings indicate why migraine management should evaluate the effectiveness of current therapy on cognitive function and address these symptoms. “Cognitive impairment is a critical component of the migraine experience,” Lipton said. “It should be routinely addressed.” Lipton advised clinicians to simply ask their patients if they experience brain fog or difficulties in reading, concentrating or engaging in complicated tasks when they have or are anticipating a headache. “Another important message is that the cognitive symptoms of migraine can be part of what we think about when we’re implementing treatment strategies,” he said. Migraine typically involves pain, nausea, photophobia and phonophobia, Lipton told Healio. “We think about all of those as targets for treatment,” he said. “When we’re formulating our treatment plans, cognitive symptoms should be one of the things we assess.” For more information: Richard B. Lipton, MD , can be reached at neurology@healio.com . Sources/Disclosures Source: Starling AJ, et al. Real-world effectiveness of eptinezumab treatment on migraine-related cognitive symptoms in participants in whom 1 prior CGRP-targeted therapy had failed: 12-month results from the INFUSE study. Presented at: American Headache Society 68 th Annual Scientific Meeting; June 4-7, 2026; Orlando. Disclosures: Starling reports no relevant financial disclosures. Presented at: American Headache Society 68 th Annual Scientific Meeting; June 4-7, 2026; Orlando. “One of the most exciting things that has happened in migraine ever is the advent of CGRP-targeted therapies,” Lipton told Healio. The prospective, non-interventional, real-world INFUSE effectiveness study began with 190 adults (88.9% women), including 38 (20%) aged 18 to 34 years, 102 (53.7%) aged 35 to 54 years and 45 (23.7%) aged 55 to 74 years. A total of 154 patients (81.1%) had been diagnosed with chronic migraine , with 47 (24.7%) reporting one failed CGRP-targeted preventive therapy, 38 (20%) reporting two failed therapies and 105 (55.3%) reporting three or more failed therapies . “If somebody started on a migraine preventive and they discontinued it, they discontinued for two main reasons,” Lipton said. “One is it either doesn’t work well enough, and so they’re dissatisfied with efficacy, or the medication causes side effects.” Lipton attributed these different outcomes to migraine’s biological heterogeneity. “People may respond well to different mechanisms or even different treatments that target a similar mechanism,” he said. “Migraine is really not a one-size-fits-all condition.” The prevalence of cognitive impairments at baseline included 178 patients (93.7%) with brain fog, 150 (78.9%) with difficulty in making decisions, 157 (82.6%) with difficulty in reading comprehension and 157 (82.6%) with difficulty in complex tasks. Percentages of patients reporting that these symptoms were “moderately” to “very” or “extremely” bothersome included 82.1% for brain fog, 64.7% for difficulty in making decisions, 60% for difficulty with reading comprehension and 62.6% for difficulty with complex tasks. “The cognitive burden of migraine is very substantial,” Lipton said. Treatment included 100 mg or 300 mg of eptinezumab every 12 weeks. Among 91 patients with data on brain fog at 12 months, 27.5% said it had “minimally” improved and 37.4% said it had “much” or “very much” improved. With 80 patients reporting 12-month data on difficulty in making decisions, 21.3% said it had minimally improved and 40% said it had “much” or “very much” improved. The 84 patients with 12-month data on difficulty with reading comprehension included 23.8% who reported they had minimally improved and 35.7% who had “much” or “very much” improved. Among 82 with 12-month data on difficulty with complex tasks, 20.7% said it had “minimally” improved and 39% said it had “much” or “very much” improved. Although the INFUSE study did not collect safety data, Lipton said, data reported through established channels indicated a positive safety profile as well. Considering the cognitive burden experienced by these patients at baseline, the researchers said these findings indicate why migraine management should evaluate the effectiveness of current therapy on cognitive function and address these symptoms. “Cognitive impairment is a critical component of the migraine experience,” Lipton said. “It should be routinely addressed.” Lipton advised clinicians to simply ask their patients if they experience brain fog or difficulties in reading, concentrating or engaging in complicated tasks when they have or are anticipating a headache. “Another important message is that the cognitive symptoms of migraine can be part of what we think about when we’re implementing treatment strategies,” he said. Migraine typically involves pain, nausea, photophobia and phonophobia, Lipton told Healio. “We think about all of those as targets for treatment,” he said. “When we’re formulating our treatment plans, cognitive symptoms should be one of the things we assess.” For more information: Richard B. Lipton, MD , can be reached at neurology@healio.com . Sources/Disclosures Source: Starling AJ, et al. Real-world effectiveness of eptinezumab treatment on migraine-related cognitive symptoms in participants in whom 1 prior CGRP-targeted therapy had failed: 12-month results from the INFUSE study. Presented at: American Headache Society 68 th Annual Scientific Meeting; June 4-7, 2026; Orlando.
th
“One of the most exciting things that has happened in migraine ever is the advent of CGRP-targeted therapies,” Lipton told Healio.
Among four monoclonal antibodies and two small molecules that target CGRPs to prevent migraine approved by the FDA, eptinezumab (Vyepti, Lundbeck) is the only intravenously administered treatment, the researchers wrote.
“In that context, understanding the real-world effectiveness of eptinezumab in people who have tried and failed other CGRP-targeting therapies is very important,” Lipton said.
The prospective, non-interventional, real-world INFUSE effectiveness study began with 190 adults (88.9% women), including 38 (20%) aged 18 to 34 years, 102 (53.7%) aged 35 to 54 years and 45 (23.7%) aged 55 to 74 years.
A total of 154 patients (81.1%) had been diagnosed with chronic migraine , with 47 (24.7%) reporting one failed CGRP-targeted preventive therapy, 38 (20%) reporting two failed therapies and 105 (55.3%) reporting three or more failed therapies .
diagnosed with chronic migraine
three or more failed therapies
“If somebody started on a migraine preventive and they discontinued it, they discontinued for two main reasons,” Lipton said. “One is it either doesn’t work well enough, and so they’re dissatisfied with efficacy, or the medication causes side effects.”
Lipton attributed these different outcomes to migraine’s biological heterogeneity.
“People may respond well to different mechanisms or even different treatments that target a similar mechanism,” he said. “Migraine is really not a one-size-fits-all condition.”
The prevalence of cognitive impairments at baseline included 178 patients (93.7%) with brain fog, 150 (78.9%) with difficulty in making decisions, 157 (82.6%) with difficulty in reading comprehension and 157 (82.6%) with difficulty in complex tasks.
Percentages of patients reporting that these symptoms were “moderately” to “very” or “extremely” bothersome included 82.1% for brain fog, 64.7% for difficulty in making decisions, 60% for difficulty with reading comprehension and 62.6% for difficulty with complex tasks.
“The cognitive burden of migraine is very substantial,” Lipton said.
Treatment included 100 mg or 300 mg of eptinezumab every 12 weeks.
Among 91 patients with data on brain fog at 12 months, 27.5% said it had “minimally” improved and 37.4% said it had “much” or “very much” improved.
With 80 patients reporting 12-month data on difficulty in making decisions, 21.3% said it had minimally improved and 40% said it had “much” or “very much” improved.
The 84 patients with 12-month data on difficulty with reading comprehension included 23.8% who reported they had minimally improved and 35.7% who had “much” or “very much” improved.
Among 82 with 12-month data on difficulty with complex tasks, 20.7% said it had “minimally” improved and 39% said it had “much” or “very much” improved.
Although the INFUSE study did not collect safety data, Lipton said, data reported through established channels indicated a positive safety profile as well.
Considering the cognitive burden experienced by these patients at baseline, the researchers said these findings indicate why migraine management should evaluate the effectiveness of current therapy on cognitive function and address these symptoms.
“Cognitive impairment is a critical component of the migraine experience,” Lipton said. “It should be routinely addressed.”
Lipton advised clinicians to simply ask their patients if they experience brain fog or difficulties in reading, concentrating or engaging in complicated tasks when they have or are anticipating a headache.
“Another important message is that the cognitive symptoms of migraine can be part of what we think about when we’re implementing treatment strategies,” he said.
Migraine typically involves pain, nausea, photophobia and phonophobia, Lipton told Healio.
“We think about all of those as targets for treatment,” he said. “When we’re formulating our treatment plans, cognitive symptoms should be one of the things we assess.”
For more information:
Richard B. Lipton, MD , can be reached at neurology@healio.com .
Richard B. Lipton, MD

articles.title: Pooled analysis shows moderate/severe exacerbation rate reduction with astegolimab in COPD
articles.text:
Close Ask Healio AI New!
Close

American Thoracic Society International Conference By Isabella Hornick Fact checked by Kristen Dowd Purpose-built for physicians Ask Learn more Read more Pooled analysis shows moderate/severe exacerbation rate reduction with astegolimab in COPD June 17, 2026 4 min read By Isabella Hornick Fact checked by Kristen Dowd Add topic to email alerts Please provide your email address to receive an email when new articles are posted on COPD . Back to Healio Save Share LinkedIn Facebook X (Twitter) Bluesky Threads Email Copy Link Key takeaways: The annualized rate of moderate/severe exacerbations was smaller with astegolimab vs. placebo. Rates were reduced with astegolimab vs. placebo regardless of smoking status and baseline blood eosinophil count. ORLANDO — Adults with COPD and frequent exacerbations had a significantly lower yearly moderate/severe exacerbation rate with astegolimab vs. placebo, according to data presented at the American Thoracic Society International Conference. These findings on astegolimab (Genentech, Roche), described as “a human [immunoglobulin]G2 monoclonal antibody that blocks ST2-mediated [interleukin]-33 activity and subsequent inflammation” in the presentation, were also simultaneously published in American Journal of Respiratory and Critical Care Medicine . Data derived from Wedzicha JA, et al. B13 JAMA/NEJM/AJRCCM pulmonary session. Presented at: American Thoracic Society International Conference; May 15-20, 2026; Orlando. “This pooled analysis showed a favorable benefit-risk profile for the two-weekly [astegolimab] dose in patients with COPD and frequent exacerbations,” Jadwiga A. Wedzicha , MD, FMedSci , professor of respiratory medicine at the National Heart and Lung Institute, Imperial College, U.K., said during her presentation. In a pooled analysis of the randomized, double-blind, placebo-controlled phase 2b ALIENTO trial and phase 3 ARNASA trial, Wedzicha and colleagues assessed 2,682 patients with COPD and frequent exacerbations (at least two moderate or severe exacerbations in a 12-month period) to determine the impact of subcutaneous 476 mg astegolimab taken every 2 or 4 weeks vs. placebo taken every 2 weeks on the annualized rate of moderate/severe exacerbations. “Importantly, [patients] were enrolled regardless of what the blood eosinophil count was, whether the patient had chronic bronchitis or not, and they could be either current or former smokers,” Wedzicha said. Wedzicha highlighted that patients in the astegolimab every 2 weeks vs. placebo group had a significant decrease in the annualized moderate/severe exacerbations rate in the ALIENTO trial (RR = 0.85; 95% CI, 0.72-1) and a nonsignificant decrease in the ARNASA trial (RR = 0.85; 95% CI, 0.72-1.01). When only considering those who received at least one dose of trial treatment (n = 2,676), the pooled 2-week astegolimab group included 892 patients (mean age, 66.76 years; 60.3% men; 33.9% current smokers; 31.3% with more than two exacerbations within 12 months before screening). The pooled 4-week astegolimab group included 896 patients (mean age, 67.1 years; 60.2% men; 34% current smokers; 31.1% with more than two exacerbations within 12 months before screening). The pooled placebo group included 888 patients (mean age, 67.14 years; 60.4% men; 34.1% current smokers; 26.7% with more than two exacerbations within 12 months before screening). Throughout the trial, all patients received optimized maintenance therapy in addition to their assigned treatment. Before the 52-week mark, 15.5% of patients discontinued trial treatment, according to the study. Over 52 weeks, researchers found a significant 14.7% decrease in the adjusted annualized rate of moderate or severe exacerbations among those receiving astegolimab every 2 weeks vs. placebo (RR = 0.85; 95% CI, 0.76-0.96). Similarly, the astegolimab every 4 weeks group had a significant 12.3% reduction in this rate when placed against the placebo group (RR = 0.88; 95% CI, 0.78-0.99). When divided according to baseline blood eosinophil count (< 150 cells/ μ L , ≥ 150 cells/ μ L , < 300 cells/ μ L and ≥ 300 cells/ μ L ), patients receiving astegolimab every 2 weeks continued to have a lower annualized exacerbation rate than placebo in each subgroup. Researchers also found reduced rates with astegolimab every 2 weeks vs. placebo in both the current and former smoking subgroups. Notable subgroups with larger decreases in the adjusted annualized rate of moderate or severe exacerbation with astegolimab every 2 weeks vs. placebo included those: aged 40 to 64 years vs. 65 years or older (RR = 0.76; 95% CI, 0.62-0.94 vs. 0.89; 95% CI, 0.77-1.03); with more than two vs. two exacerbations (RR = 0.79; 95% CI, 0.66-0.93 vs. 0.88; 95% CI, 0.75-1.03); without vs. with baseline chronic bronchitis (RR = 0.78; 95% CI, 0.66-0.92 vs. 0.94; 95% CI, 0.79-1.11); and with moderate vs. severe or very severe airflow obstruction ( Global Initiative for Chronic Obstructive Lung Disease [GOLD] stage 2 vs. 3 or 4; RR = 0.73; 95% CI, 0.57-0.92 vs. 0.88; 95% CI, 0.75-1.04 and 1; 95% CI, 0.81-1.23). When evaluating the annualized rate of just severe exacerbations, Wedzicha highlighted a 32% decrease in the adjusted rate among those receiving astegolimab every 2 weeks vs. placebo (RR = 0.68; 95% CI, 0.52-0.87), but this was only nominally significant. Of the other six secondary endpoints, two were nominally significant with astegolimab every 2 weeks vs. placebo: annualized rate of moderate/severe exacerbations in those with a blood eosinophil count less than 300 cells/ μL (adjusted rate reduction, 13%; P = .0312) and odds for a decrease of at least four points in St. George’s Respiratory Questionnaire for COPD from baseline to week 52 (OR = 1.35; 95% CI, 1.11-1.64; P = .0029). In terms of safety, the 2-week astegolimab group had the lowest adverse event rate per 100 patient-years (365.19), followed by the 4-week astegolimab group (402.96) and the placebo group (408.39). This pattern was also found for serious adverse events (2-week astegolimab, 38.92; 4-week astegolimab, 41.45; placebo, 47.96). Wedzicha noted that the event rates for infections and major adverse cardiovascular events were comparable between the astegolimab groups and the placebo group. “The safety profile was very good,” Wedzicha said. Sources/Disclosures Source: Wedzicha JA, et al. B13 JAMA/NEJM/AJRCCM pulmonary session. Presented at: American Thoracic Society International Conference; May 15-20, 2026; Orlando. Reference: Wedzicha JA, et al. Am J Respir Crit Care Med . 2026;doi:10.1093/ajrccm/aamag226 . Disclosures: F. Hoffman-La Roche and Genentech sponsored this study. Wedzicha reports receiving grants from AstraZeneca, Boehringer Ingelheim, Chiesi, GlaxoSmithKline and Novartis; receiving honoraria/consulting fees from Altesa, AstraZeneca, Boehringer Ingelheim, CSL Behring, Empirico, EpiEndo, F. Hoffman-La Roche, Gilead, GlaxoSmithKline, Handok, MucPharm, NEATstik, Novartis, Pfizer, Recipharm and Sanofi; and participating in a data safety monitoring board for Virtus. In terms of safety, the 2-week astegolimab group had the lowest adverse event rate per 100 patient-years (365.19), followed by the 4-week astegolimab group (402.96) and the placebo group (408.39). This pattern was also found for serious adverse events (2-week astegolimab, 38.92; 4-week astegolimab, 41.45; placebo, 47.96). Wedzicha noted that the event rates for infections and major adverse cardiovascular events were comparable between the astegolimab groups and the placebo group. “The safety profile was very good,” Wedzicha said. Back to Healio Save Share LinkedIn Facebook X (Twitter) Bluesky Threads Email Copy Link Key takeaways: The annualized rate of moderate/severe exacerbations was smaller with astegolimab vs. placebo. Rates were reduced with astegolimab vs. placebo regardless of smoking status and baseline blood eosinophil count. ORLANDO — Adults with COPD and frequent exacerbations had a significantly lower yearly moderate/severe exacerbation rate with astegolimab vs. placebo, according to data presented at the American Thoracic Society International Conference. These findings on astegolimab (Genentech, Roche), described as “a human [immunoglobulin]G2 monoclonal antibody that blocks ST2-mediated [interleukin]-33 activity and subsequent inflammation” in the presentation, were also simultaneously published in American Journal of Respiratory and Critical Care Medicine . Data derived from Wedzicha JA, et al. B13 JAMA/NEJM/AJRCCM pulmonary session. Presented at: American Thoracic Society International Conference; May 15-20, 2026; Orlando. “This pooled analysis showed a favorable benefit-risk profile for the two-weekly [astegolimab] dose in patients with COPD and frequent exacerbations,” Jadwiga A. Wedzicha , MD, FMedSci , professor of respiratory medicine at the National Heart and Lung Institute, Imperial College, U.K., said during her presentation. In a pooled analysis of the randomized, double-blind, placebo-controlled phase 2b ALIENTO trial and phase 3 ARNASA trial, Wedzicha and colleagues assessed 2,682 patients with COPD and frequent exacerbations (at least two moderate or severe exacerbations in a 12-month period) to determine the impact of subcutaneous 476 mg astegolimab taken every 2 or 4 weeks vs. placebo taken every 2 weeks on the annualized rate of moderate/severe exacerbations. “Importantly, [patients] were enrolled regardless of what the blood eosinophil count was, whether the patient had chronic bronchitis or not, and they could be either current or former smokers,” Wedzicha said. Wedzicha highlighted that patients in the astegolimab every 2 weeks vs. placebo group had a significant decrease in the annualized moderate/severe exacerbations rate in the ALIENTO trial (RR = 0.85; 95% CI, 0.72-1) and a nonsignificant decrease in the ARNASA trial (RR = 0.85; 95% CI, 0.72-1.01). When only considering those who received at least one dose of trial treatment (n = 2,676), the pooled 2-week astegolimab group included 892 patients (mean age, 66.76 years; 60.3% men; 33.9% current smokers; 31.3% with more than two exacerbations within 12 months before screening). The pooled 4-week astegolimab group included 896 patients (mean age, 67.1 years; 60.2% men; 34% current smokers; 31.1% with more than two exacerbations within 12 months before screening). The pooled placebo group included 888 patients (mean age, 67.14 years; 60.4% men; 34.1% current smokers; 26.7% with more than two exacerbations within 12 months before screening). Throughout the trial, all patients received optimized maintenance therapy in addition to their assigned treatment. Before the 52-week mark, 15.5% of patients discontinued trial treatment, according to the study. Over 52 weeks, researchers found a significant 14.7% decrease in the adjusted annualized rate of moderate or severe exacerbations among those receiving astegolimab every 2 weeks vs. placebo (RR = 0.85; 95% CI, 0.76-0.96). Similarly, the astegolimab every 4 weeks group had a significant 12.3% reduction in this rate when placed against the placebo group (RR = 0.88; 95% CI, 0.78-0.99). When divided according to baseline blood eosinophil count (< 150 cells/ μ L , ≥ 150 cells/ μ L , < 300 cells/ μ L and ≥ 300 cells/ μ L ), patients receiving astegolimab every 2 weeks continued to have a lower annualized exacerbation rate than placebo in each subgroup. Researchers also found reduced rates with astegolimab every 2 weeks vs. placebo in both the current and former smoking subgroups. Notable subgroups with larger decreases in the adjusted annualized rate of moderate or severe exacerbation with astegolimab every 2 weeks vs. placebo included those: aged 40 to 64 years vs. 65 years or older (RR = 0.76; 95% CI, 0.62-0.94 vs. 0.89; 95% CI, 0.77-1.03); with more than two vs. two exacerbations (RR = 0.79; 95% CI, 0.66-0.93 vs. 0.88; 95% CI, 0.75-1.03); without vs. with baseline chronic bronchitis (RR = 0.78; 95% CI, 0.66-0.92 vs. 0.94; 95% CI, 0.79-1.11); and with moderate vs. severe or very severe airflow obstruction ( Global Initiative for Chronic Obstructive Lung Disease [GOLD] stage 2 vs. 3 or 4; RR = 0.73; 95% CI, 0.57-0.92 vs. 0.88; 95% CI, 0.75-1.04 and 1; 95% CI, 0.81-1.23). When evaluating the annualized rate of just severe exacerbations, Wedzicha highlighted a 32% decrease in the adjusted rate among those receiving astegolimab every 2 weeks vs. placebo (RR = 0.68; 95% CI, 0.52-0.87), but this was only nominally significant. Of the other six secondary endpoints, two were nominally significant with astegolimab every 2 weeks vs. placebo: annualized rate of moderate/severe exacerbations in those with a blood eosinophil count less than 300 cells/ μL (adjusted rate reduction, 13%; P = .0312) and odds for a decrease of at least four points in St. George’s Respiratory Questionnaire for COPD from baseline to week 52 (OR = 1.35; 95% CI, 1.11-1.64; P = .0029). In terms of safety, the 2-week astegolimab group had the lowest adverse event rate per 100 patient-years (365.19), followed by the 4-week astegolimab group (402.96) and the placebo group (408.39). This pattern was also found for serious adverse events (2-week astegolimab, 38.92; 4-week astegolimab, 41.45; placebo, 47.96). Wedzicha noted that the event rates for infections and major adverse cardiovascular events were comparable between the astegolimab groups and the placebo group. “The safety profile was very good,” Wedzicha said. Reference: Wedzicha JA, et al. Am J Respir Crit Care Med . 2026;doi:10.1093/ajrccm/aamag226 . Disclosures: F. Hoffman-La Roche and Genentech sponsored this study. Key takeaways:
The annualized rate of moderate/severe exacerbations was smaller with astegolimab vs. placebo. Rates were reduced with astegolimab vs. placebo regardless of smoking status and baseline blood eosinophil count.
The annualized rate of moderate/severe exacerbations was smaller with astegolimab vs. placebo.
Rates were reduced with astegolimab vs. placebo regardless of smoking status and baseline blood eosinophil count.
ORLANDO — Adults with COPD and frequent exacerbations had a significantly lower yearly moderate/severe exacerbation rate with astegolimab vs. placebo, according to data presented at the American Thoracic Society International Conference.
These findings on astegolimab (Genentech, Roche), described as “a human [immunoglobulin]G2 monoclonal antibody that blocks ST2-mediated [interleukin]-33 activity and subsequent inflammation” in the presentation, were also simultaneously published in American Journal of Respiratory and Critical Care Medicine .
American Journal of Respiratory and Critical Care Medicine
Data derived from Wedzicha JA, et al. B13 JAMA/NEJM/AJRCCM pulmonary session. Presented at: American Thoracic Society International Conference; May 15-20, 2026; Orlando.
“This pooled analysis showed a favorable benefit-risk profile for the two-weekly [astegolimab] dose in patients with COPD and frequent exacerbations,” Jadwiga A. Wedzicha , MD, FMedSci , professor of respiratory medicine at the National Heart and Lung Institute, Imperial College, U.K., said during her presentation.
Jadwiga A.
Wedzicha
, MD,
FMedSci
,
In a pooled analysis of the randomized, double-blind, placebo-controlled phase 2b ALIENTO trial and phase 3 ARNASA trial, Wedzicha and colleagues assessed 2,682 patients with COPD and frequent exacerbations (at least two moderate or severe exacerbations in a 12-month period) to determine the impact of subcutaneous 476 mg astegolimab taken every 2 or 4 weeks vs. placebo taken every 2 weeks on the annualized rate of moderate/severe exacerbations.
frequent exacerbations
“Importantly, [patients] were enrolled regardless of what the blood eosinophil count was, whether the patient had chronic bronchitis or not, and they could be either current or former smokers,” Wedzicha said.
Wedzicha highlighted that patients in the astegolimab every 2 weeks vs. placebo group had a significant decrease in the annualized moderate/severe exacerbations rate in the ALIENTO trial (RR = 0.85; 95% CI, 0.72-1) and a nonsignificant decrease in the ARNASA trial (RR = 0.85; 95% CI, 0.72-1.01).
When only considering those who received at least one dose of trial treatment (n = 2,676), the pooled 2-week astegolimab group included 892 patients (mean age, 66.76 years; 60.3% men; 33.9% current smokers; 31.3% with more than two exacerbations within 12 months before screening). The pooled 4-week astegolimab group included 896 patients (mean age, 67.1 years; 60.2% men; 34% current smokers; 31.1% with more than two exacerbations within 12 months before screening). The pooled placebo group included 888 patients (mean age, 67.14 years; 60.4% men; 34.1% current smokers; 26.7% with more than two exacerbations within 12 months before screening).
Throughout the trial, all patients received optimized maintenance therapy in addition to their assigned treatment.
Before the 52-week mark, 15.5% of patients discontinued trial treatment, according to the study.
Over 52 weeks, researchers found a significant 14.7% decrease in the adjusted annualized rate of moderate or severe exacerbations among those receiving astegolimab every 2 weeks vs. placebo (RR = 0.85; 95% CI, 0.76-0.96). Similarly, the astegolimab every 4 weeks group had a significant 12.3% reduction in this rate when placed against the placebo group (RR = 0.88; 95% CI, 0.78-0.99).
When divided according to baseline blood eosinophil count (< 150 cells/ μ L , ≥ 150 cells/ μ L , < 300 cells/ μ L and ≥ 300 cells/ μ L ), patients receiving astegolimab every 2 weeks continued to have a lower annualized exacerbation rate than placebo in each subgroup.
μ
L
≥
Researchers also found reduced rates with astegolimab every 2 weeks vs. placebo in both the current and former smoking subgroups.
Notable subgroups with larger decreases in the adjusted annualized rate of moderate or severe exacerbation with astegolimab every 2 weeks vs. placebo included those:
aged 40 to 64 years vs. 65 years or older (RR = 0.76; 95% CI, 0.62-0.94 vs. 0.89; 95% CI, 0.77-1.03); with more than two vs. two exacerbations (RR = 0.79; 95% CI, 0.66-0.93 vs. 0.88; 95% CI, 0.75-1.03); without vs. with baseline chronic bronchitis (RR = 0.78; 95% CI, 0.66-0.92 vs. 0.94; 95% CI, 0.79-1.11); and with moderate vs. severe or very severe airflow obstruction ( Global Initiative for Chronic Obstructive Lung Disease [GOLD] stage 2 vs. 3 or 4; RR = 0.73; 95% CI, 0.57-0.92 vs. 0.88; 95% CI, 0.75-1.04 and 1; 95% CI, 0.81-1.23).
aged 40 to 64 years vs. 65 years or older (RR = 0.76; 95% CI, 0.62-0.94 vs. 0.89; 95% CI, 0.77-1.03);
with more than two vs. two exacerbations (RR = 0.79; 95% CI, 0.66-0.93 vs. 0.88; 95% CI, 0.75-1.03);
without vs. with baseline chronic bronchitis (RR = 0.78; 95% CI, 0.66-0.92 vs. 0.94; 95% CI, 0.79-1.11); and
with moderate vs. severe or very severe airflow obstruction ( Global Initiative for Chronic Obstructive Lung Disease [GOLD] stage 2 vs. 3 or 4; RR = 0.73; 95% CI, 0.57-0.92 vs. 0.88; 95% CI, 0.75-1.04 and 1; 95% CI, 0.81-1.23).
Global Initiative for Chronic Obstructive Lung Disease
When evaluating the annualized rate of just severe exacerbations, Wedzicha highlighted a 32% decrease in the adjusted rate among those receiving astegolimab every 2 weeks vs. placebo (RR = 0.68; 95% CI, 0.52-0.87), but this was only nominally significant.
Of the other six secondary endpoints, two were nominally significant with astegolimab every 2 weeks vs. placebo: annualized rate of moderate/severe exacerbations in those with a blood eosinophil count less than 300 cells/ μL (adjusted rate reduction, 13%; P = .0312) and odds for a decrease of at least four points in St. George’s Respiratory Questionnaire for COPD from baseline to week 52 (OR = 1.35; 95% CI, 1.11-1.64; P = .0029).
μL
P
In terms of safety, the 2-week astegolimab group had the lowest adverse event rate per 100 patient-years (365.19), followed by the 4-week astegolimab group (402.96) and the placebo group (408.39). This pattern was also found for serious adverse events (2-week astegolimab, 38.92; 4-week astegolimab, 41.45; placebo, 47.96).
Wedzicha noted that the event rates for infections and major adverse cardiovascular events were comparable between the astegolimab groups and the placebo group.
“The safety profile was very good,” Wedzicha said.

articles.title: Burosumab improves physical function, reduces pain in X-linked hypophosphatemia
articles.text:
Close Ask Healio AI New!
Close

The Endocrine Society By Michael Monostra Fact checked by Richard Smith Purpose-built for physicians Ask Learn more Read more Burosumab improves physical function, reduces pain in X-linked hypophosphatemia June 16, 2026 2 min read By Michael Monostra Fact checked by Richard Smith Add topic to email alerts Please provide your email address to receive an email when new articles are posted on Bone/Mineral Metabolism . Subscribe Added to email alerts You've successfully added Bone/Mineral Metabolism to your alerts. You will receive an email when new content is published. Back to Healio Save Share LinkedIn Facebook X (Twitter) Bluesky Threads Email Copy Link Key takeaways: Patients with X-linked hypophosphatemia reported reduced pain and stiffness with burosumab at 3 years. No improvements in any questionnaire items were seen with vitamin D and phosphate salts. CHICAGO — Burosumab is tied to greater improvements in pain, stiffness and physical function than active vitamin D and phosphate salts among patients with X-linked hypophosphatemia, according to data presented at ENDO 2026. Burosumab-twza (Crysvita, Kyowa Kirin), a monoclonal anti-fibroblast growth factor 23 antibody, was approved by the FDA as the first therapy to treat X-linked hypophosphatemia, a rare form of rickets, in 2018. In data from a prospective observational study, researchers assessed how burosumab impacted quality of life among people with X-linked hypophosphatemia compared with treatment with vitamin D or phosphate salts in a real-world setting. Burosumab conferred greater improvements in all three domains of the Western Ontario and McMaster Universities Osteoarthritis Index than active vitamin D and phosphate salts for patients with X-linked hypophosphatemia. “This analysis provides insights into burosumab’s impact on activities of daily living across three [Western Ontario and McMaster Universities Osteoarthritis Index] domains in 3 years,” Erik A. Imel, MD, professor of medicine and pediatrics at Indiana University School of Medicine, said during a presentation. “Treatment of burosumab did improve in the domains of pain, stiffness and physical function compared to phosphate and vitamin D.” Researchers analyzed data from the X-linked Hypophosphatemia Disease Monitoring Program from July 2018 to February 2025. Data were collected from 54 participants receiving burosumab (mean age, 40 years; 70.4% women) and 55 patients receiving active vitamin D and phosphate salts (mean age, 39.5 years; 81.8% women). Follow-up data for the burosumab group was collected beginning 1 year after drug initiation. Researchers used the Western Ontario and McMaster Universities Osteoarthritis Index (WOMAC) questionnaire to assess self-reported pain, stiffness and physical function, with higher scores indicating worse symptoms. Follow-up continued for up to 3 years. At baseline, the burosumab groups had higher scores for pain (mean score, 39 points vs. 28.8 points; P = .031) and stiffness (mean score, 51.2 points vs. 39.1 points; P = .023) than the vitamin D with phosphate salts group. No difference was seen in total score or physical function score. Pain scores decreased by 9.8 points at 3 years with burosumab compared with a 3.3-point increase for the vitamin D and phosphate salt group ( P < .001). Patients receiving burosumab had a 15.2-point decrease in stiffness score at 3 years compared with a 3.7-point decline for those receiving vitamin D and phosphate salts ( P = .016). Physical function scores dropped by 10 points at 3 years compared with a 3.3-point increase with vitamin D or phosphate salts ( P = .001). The burosumab group had a larger increase in serum phosphate than the vitamin D and phosphate salts group at 1 year that persisted at 3 years. When individual questions on the WOMAC questionnaire were assessed, patients receiving burosumab had improvements in 17 of the 24 items, while no improvements were seen with vitamin D and phosphate salts. When responses to individual questions were classified into five categories ranging from “none” to “severe,” Imel noted that most participants receiving burosumab had a reduction in symptom severity over time on multiple items. “Pain walking on a flat surface, you can see a notable shift in the distribution toward the lower scores in the patients on burosumab, and there was not a similar change in distribution with the phosphate and vitamin D scores,” Imel said. “For pain standing upright, again, you have a similar change in shifting toward the zero to two grade scores overall with burosumab, and no significant change with phosphate and vitamin D. It’s going to be a big theme here.” Imel said some of the questionnaire items in which burosumab was not associated with improvements were going up and down stairs, heavy chores, nocturnal symptoms, and putting on or taking off socks or stockings. Imel added that one of the study limitations was that individual item scores were not compared between the burosumab and vitamin D and phosphate groups. Published by: Sources/Disclosures Source: Imel EA, et al. Abstract ORF08-05. Presented at: ENDO annual meeting; June 13-16, 2026; Chicago. Back to Healio Save Share LinkedIn Facebook X (Twitter) Bluesky Threads Email Copy Link Key takeaways: Patients with X-linked hypophosphatemia reported reduced pain and stiffness with burosumab at 3 years. No improvements in any questionnaire items were seen with vitamin D and phosphate salts. CHICAGO — Burosumab is tied to greater improvements in pain, stiffness and physical function than active vitamin D and phosphate salts among patients with X-linked hypophosphatemia, according to data presented at ENDO 2026. Burosumab-twza (Crysvita, Kyowa Kirin), a monoclonal anti-fibroblast growth factor 23 antibody, was approved by the FDA as the first therapy to treat X-linked hypophosphatemia, a rare form of rickets, in 2018. In data from a prospective observational study, researchers assessed how burosumab impacted quality of life among people with X-linked hypophosphatemia compared with treatment with vitamin D or phosphate salts in a real-world setting. Burosumab conferred greater improvements in all three domains of the Western Ontario and McMaster Universities Osteoarthritis Index than active vitamin D and phosphate salts for patients with X-linked hypophosphatemia. “This analysis provides insights into burosumab’s impact on activities of daily living across three [Western Ontario and McMaster Universities Osteoarthritis Index] domains in 3 years,” Erik A. Imel, MD, professor of medicine and pediatrics at Indiana University School of Medicine, said during a presentation. “Treatment of burosumab did improve in the domains of pain, stiffness and physical function compared to phosphate and vitamin D.” Researchers analyzed data from the X-linked Hypophosphatemia Disease Monitoring Program from July 2018 to February 2025. Data were collected from 54 participants receiving burosumab (mean age, 40 years; 70.4% women) and 55 patients receiving active vitamin D and phosphate salts (mean age, 39.5 years; 81.8% women). Follow-up data for the burosumab group was collected beginning 1 year after drug initiation. Researchers used the Western Ontario and McMaster Universities Osteoarthritis Index (WOMAC) questionnaire to assess self-reported pain, stiffness and physical function, with higher scores indicating worse symptoms. Follow-up continued for up to 3 years. At baseline, the burosumab groups had higher scores for pain (mean score, 39 points vs. 28.8 points; P = .031) and stiffness (mean score, 51.2 points vs. 39.1 points; P = .023) than the vitamin D with phosphate salts group. No difference was seen in total score or physical function score. Pain scores decreased by 9.8 points at 3 years with burosumab compared with a 3.3-point increase for the vitamin D and phosphate salt group ( P < .001). Patients receiving burosumab had a 15.2-point decrease in stiffness score at 3 years compared with a 3.7-point decline for those receiving vitamin D and phosphate salts ( P = .016). Physical function scores dropped by 10 points at 3 years compared with a 3.3-point increase with vitamin D or phosphate salts ( P = .001). The burosumab group had a larger increase in serum phosphate than the vitamin D and phosphate salts group at 1 year that persisted at 3 years. When individual questions on the WOMAC questionnaire were assessed, patients receiving burosumab had improvements in 17 of the 24 items, while no improvements were seen with vitamin D and phosphate salts. When responses to individual questions were classified into five categories ranging from “none” to “severe,” Imel noted that most participants receiving burosumab had a reduction in symptom severity over time on multiple items. “Pain walking on a flat surface, you can see a notable shift in the distribution toward the lower scores in the patients on burosumab, and there was not a similar change in distribution with the phosphate and vitamin D scores,” Imel said. “For pain standing upright, again, you have a similar change in shifting toward the zero to two grade scores overall with burosumab, and no significant change with phosphate and vitamin D. It’s going to be a big theme here.” Imel said some of the questionnaire items in which burosumab was not associated with improvements were going up and down stairs, heavy chores, nocturnal symptoms, and putting on or taking off socks or stockings. Imel added that one of the study limitations was that individual item scores were not compared between the burosumab and vitamin D and phosphate groups. Published by: Sources/Disclosures Source: Imel EA, et al. Abstract ORF08-05. Presented at: ENDO annual meeting; June 13-16, 2026; Chicago. Key takeaways:
Patients with X-linked hypophosphatemia reported reduced pain and stiffness with burosumab at 3 years. No improvements in any questionnaire items were seen with vitamin D and phosphate salts.
Patients with X-linked hypophosphatemia reported reduced pain and stiffness with burosumab at 3 years.
No improvements in any questionnaire items were seen with vitamin D and phosphate salts.
CHICAGO — Burosumab is tied to greater improvements in pain, stiffness and physical function than active vitamin D and phosphate salts among patients with X-linked hypophosphatemia, according to data presented at ENDO 2026.
X-linked hypophosphatemia,
Burosumab-twza (Crysvita, Kyowa Kirin), a monoclonal anti-fibroblast growth factor 23 antibody, was approved by the FDA as the first therapy to treat X-linked hypophosphatemia, a rare form of rickets, in 2018. In data from a prospective observational study, researchers assessed how burosumab impacted quality of life among people with X-linked hypophosphatemia compared with treatment with vitamin D or phosphate salts in a real-world setting.
FDA as the first therapy
Burosumab conferred greater improvements in all three domains of the Western Ontario and McMaster Universities Osteoarthritis Index than active vitamin D and phosphate salts for patients with X-linked hypophosphatemia.
Western Ontario and McMaster Universities Osteoarthritis Index than active vitamin D and phosphate salts for patients with X-linked hypophosphatemia.
active vitamin D and phosphate salts for patients with X-linked hypophosphatemia.
X-linked hypophosphatemia.
“This analysis provides insights into burosumab’s impact on activities of daily living across three [Western Ontario and McMaster Universities Osteoarthritis Index] domains in 3 years,” Erik A. Imel, MD, professor of medicine and pediatrics at Indiana University School of Medicine, said during a presentation. “Treatment of burosumab did improve in the domains of pain, stiffness and physical function compared to phosphate and vitamin D.”
Erik A. Imel, MD,
Researchers analyzed data from the X-linked Hypophosphatemia Disease Monitoring Program from July 2018 to February 2025. Data were collected from 54 participants receiving burosumab (mean age, 40 years; 70.4% women) and 55 patients receiving active vitamin D and phosphate salts (mean age, 39.5 years; 81.8% women). Follow-up data for the burosumab group was collected beginning 1 year after drug initiation. Researchers used the Western Ontario and McMaster Universities Osteoarthritis Index (WOMAC) questionnaire to assess self-reported pain, stiffness and physical function, with higher scores indicating worse symptoms. Follow-up continued for up to 3 years.
At baseline, the burosumab groups had higher scores for pain (mean score, 39 points vs. 28.8 points; P = .031) and stiffness (mean score, 51.2 points vs. 39.1 points; P = .023) than the vitamin D with phosphate salts group. No difference was seen in total score or physical function score.
P
Pain scores decreased by 9.8 points at 3 years with burosumab compared with a 3.3-point increase for the vitamin D and phosphate salt group ( P < .001). Patients receiving burosumab had a 15.2-point decrease in stiffness score at 3 years compared with a 3.7-point decline for those receiving vitamin D and phosphate salts ( P = .016). Physical function scores dropped by 10 points at 3 years compared with a 3.3-point increase with vitamin D or phosphate salts ( P = .001).
The burosumab group had a larger increase in serum phosphate than the vitamin D and phosphate salts group at 1 year that persisted at 3 years.
When individual questions on the WOMAC questionnaire were assessed, patients receiving burosumab had improvements in 17 of the 24 items, while no improvements were seen with vitamin D and phosphate salts.
When responses to individual questions were classified into five categories ranging from “none” to “severe,” Imel noted that most participants receiving burosumab had a reduction in symptom severity over time on multiple items.
“Pain walking on a flat surface, you can see a notable shift in the distribution toward the lower scores in the patients on burosumab, and there was not a similar change in distribution with the phosphate and vitamin D scores,” Imel said. “For pain standing upright, again, you have a similar change in shifting toward the zero to two grade scores overall with burosumab, and no significant change with phosphate and vitamin D. It’s going to be a big theme here.”
Imel said some of the questionnaire items in which burosumab was not associated with improvements were going up and down stairs, heavy chores, nocturnal symptoms, and putting on or taking off socks or stockings. Imel added that one of the study limitations was that individual item scores were not compared between the burosumab and vitamin D and phosphate groups.
Published by: Sources/Disclosures Source: Imel EA, et al. Abstract ORF08-05. Presented at: ENDO annual meeting; June 13-16, 2026; Chicago. Disclosures: Imel reports advising for Amgen, Kyowa Kirin, Neurocrine Biosciences and UltraGenyx; serving as a clinical trial investigator for Calcilytix, Kyowa Kirin, Neurocrine Biosciences and UltraGenyx; and consulting for Kyowa Kirin and UltraGenyx. articles.title: Ebola cases increase almost 40% in a week as death toll passes 200
articles.text:
Skip to Main Content
Health Ebola cases increase almost 40% in a week as death toll passes 200 There are 894 confirmed cases so far in 32 health zones across eastern Congo Manage alerts for this article Email this article Share this article A health worker disinfects the area around a treatment center under construction at the General Hospital in Bunia, Congo, on Monday. Moses Sawasawa/AP By Associated Press June 18, 2026 DAKAR, Senegal — The Ebola outbreak in Congo and Uganda has claimed more than 200 lives in its first month and is the worst known outbreak at this stage, with up to 35,000 suspected potential contacts, Africa’s Centres for Disease Control and Prevention said on Thursday. With 894 confirmed cases so far, the current outbreak is three times worse than a previous outbreak in Uganda in 2000, which had 281 cases at the same point, said Dr. Wessam Mankoula, a medical epidemiologist at Africa CDC. Advertisement The latest number of cases is believed to be higher because the outbreak was confirmed on May 15, weeks after it was suspected to have begun. The number of cases has increased 38% since last week and is now in 32 health zones across eastern Congo, said Mankoula. global health , infectious disease , public health Submit a correction request Reprints Associated Press Newsletter Understand how science, health policy, and medicine shape the world every day Advertisement Most Popular Eli Lilly ends 340B drug discounts to some hospitals for failing to provide claims data Covid vaccination cut risk of adverse heart events, large study finds I’m an Alzheimer’s specialist. articles.title: CD47 Antibody Combo Shows Meaningful Activity in Previously Untreated AML
articles.text:
Advertisement x
Advertisement
x
Advertisement News | Articles | June 16, 2026 CD47 Antibody Combo Shows Meaningful Activity in Previously Untreated AML Author(s) Russ Conroy Fact checked by: Roman Fabbricatore Interact with this article Ask Scout! Ask questions, get summaries, explore topics. Instantly. Data from a phase 2 trial support further clinical development of AK117 in previously untreated acute myeloid leukemia. Advertisement “AK117 combined with [azacitidine/venetoclax] demonstrated clinically meaningful efficacy with improved response quality, durable remission, and favorable survival trends. All these efficiency benefits were achieved with a manageable and comparable safety profile,” according to study investigator Huafeng Wang. Combining the investigational anti-CD47 antibody AK117 with azacitidine (Vidaza) and venetoclax (Venclexta) demonstrated meaningful activity vs placebo plus azacitidine/venetoclax among patients with previously untreated acute myeloid leukemia (AML) who were ineligible for intensive chemotherapy, according to findings from a phase 2 trial (NCT06387420) presented at the European Hematology Association (EHA) 2026 Congress. 1 Data showed an overall response rate (ORR) of 80.0% (95% CI, 61.4%-92.3%) in the AK117 arm compared with 66.7% (95% CI, 47.2%-82.7%) in the placebo arm. The composite complete response (CRc) rates were 56.7% (95% CI, 37.4%-74.5%) vs 53.3% (95% CI, 34.3%-71.7%) in each respective arm, and the minimal residual disease (MRD)–negative CRc rates were 46.7% (95% CI, 28.3%-65.7%) vs 36.7% (95% CI, 19.9%-56.1%). Additionally, the median duration of response (DOR) was 10.4 months (95% CI, 5.3-not evaluable [NE]) vs 5.6 months (95% CI, 3.8-6.8) in each arm. After a median follow-up of 10.0 months in the AK117 arm and 8.8 months in the placebo arm, the median overall survival (OS) was not reached (NR; 95% CI, 9.1-NE) vs 8.3 months (95% CI, 7.5-NE) in each arm (HR, 0.46; 95% CI, 0.20-1.06). The OS rates in each arm were 83.3% (95% CI, 64.5%-92.7%) vs 73.2% (95% CI, 53.4%-85.6%) at 6 months and 78.7% (95% CI, 58.2%-89.9%) vs 43.1% (95% CI, 22.0%-62.6%) at 9 months. The median event-free survival (EFS) was 9.1 months (95% CI, 5.8-11.4) vs 6.9 months (95% CI, 4.2-8.2) in each arm (HR, 0.46; 95% CI, 0.22-0.94), with respective 6-month EFS rates of 67.8% (95% CI, 45.3%-82.6%) vs 55.5% (95% CI, 32.5%-73.4%) and 9-month rates of 53.2% (95% CI, 31.1%-71.2%) vs 14.1% (95% CI, 2.4%-35.5%). “AK117 combined with [azacitidine/venetoclax] demonstrated clinically meaningful efficacy with improved response quality, durable remission, and favorable survival trends. All these efficiency benefits were achieved with a manageable and comparable safety profile,” study investigator Huafeng Wang, from The First Affiliated Hospital and Zhejiang University School of Medicine in Hangzhou, China, stated in the presentation. “This [study] supports AK117 as a promising addition to the [azacitidine/venetoclax] backbone, but it still needs further clinical development in this population.” AK117 was engineered as a next-generation, humanized IgG4 monoclonal antibody directed towards CD47. Both arms of the agent bind to CD47 on the same red blood cell (RBC) in a way that precludes RBC bridging and hemagglutination due to unique binding geometry, necessitating a priming dose to manage anemia. 2 In the phase 2 study, patients were randomly assigned 1:1 to receive AK117 plus azacitidine/venetoclax (n = 30) or placebo in combination with azacitidine/venetoclax (n = 30). Across each 4-week cycle, patients received AK117 or placebo at 20 mg/kg intravenously on day 1 every 2 weeks; venetoclax at 100 mg on day 1, 200 mg on day 2 of cycle, and 400 mg daily thereafter every 4 weeks; and azacitidine at 75 mg/m 2 subcutaneously on days 1 to 7 every 4 weeks. The trial’s primary end points were safety and CRc rate per ELN 2022 criteria. Secondary end points included ORR, MRD-negative CRc, duration of CRc, EFS, and OS. Patients 18 years and older with newly diagnosed AML ineligible for intensive chemotherapy, an ECOG performance status of 3 or lower or 2 or lower if 75 years or older, and adequate organ function were eligible for enrollment on the trial. The median patient age was 61.6 years (range, 23.7-74.4) in the AK117 arm and 64.0 years (range, 22.2-76.4) in the placebo arm; 50.0% vs 43.3% of patients in each arm were female. Most patients in each arm had an ECOG performance status of 2 (76.7% vs 83.3%), and favorable risk per ELN 2024 guidelines (50.0% vs 53.3%). All patients experienced treatment-emergent adverse effects (TEAEs) of any grade. TEAEs of grade 3 or higher occurred in 96.7% of the AK117 arm and 93.3% of the placebo arm, and 66.7% vs 66.7% in each group experienced serious AEs. Three patients in the experimental arm discontinued treatment due to TEAEs compared with 2 in the placebo arm. Additionally, fatal TEAEs were reported in 16.7% and 30.0% of each respective arm. The most common TEAEs in the AK117 and placebo arms, respectively, included decreased platelet counts (86.7% vs 66.7%), decreased white blood cell counts (83.3% vs 83.3%), decreased absolute neutrophil counts (80.0% vs 76.7%), and anemia (46.7% vs 50.0%). References Jin J, Zhang L, Zhang Q, et al. A phase II randomized study of AK117 versus placebo in combination with venetoclax and azacitidine in previously untreated acute myeloid leukemia patients ineligible for intensive chemotherapy. Presented at: European Hematology Association 2026 Congress; June 11-14, 2026; Stockholm, Sweden. Abstract S131. Qu T, Zhong T, Pang X, et al. Ligufalimab, a novel anti-CD47 antibody with no hemagglutination demonstrates both monotherapy and combo antitumor activity. J Immunother Cancer . 2022;10(11):e005517. doi:10.1136/jitc-2022-005517 Newsletter Stay up to date on recent advances in the multidisciplinary approach to cancer. News | Articles | June 16, 2026 CD47 Antibody Combo Shows Meaningful Activity in Previously Untreated AML Author(s) Russ Conroy Fact checked by: Roman Fabbricatore Interact with this article Ask Scout! Ask questions, get summaries, explore topics. Instantly. Data from a phase 2 trial support further clinical development of AK117 in previously untreated acute myeloid leukemia. Advertisement “AK117 combined with [azacitidine/venetoclax] demonstrated clinically meaningful efficacy with improved response quality, durable remission, and favorable survival trends. All these efficiency benefits were achieved with a manageable and comparable safety profile,” according to study investigator Huafeng Wang. Combining the investigational anti-CD47 antibody AK117 with azacitidine (Vidaza) and venetoclax (Venclexta) demonstrated meaningful activity vs placebo plus azacitidine/venetoclax among patients with previously untreated acute myeloid leukemia (AML) who were ineligible for intensive chemotherapy, according to findings from a phase 2 trial (NCT06387420) presented at the European Hematology Association (EHA) 2026 Congress. 1 Data showed an overall response rate (ORR) of 80.0% (95% CI, 61.4%-92.3%) in the AK117 arm compared with 66.7% (95% CI, 47.2%-82.7%) in the placebo arm. The composite complete response (CRc) rates were 56.7% (95% CI, 37.4%-74.5%) vs 53.3% (95% CI, 34.3%-71.7%) in each respective arm, and the minimal residual disease (MRD)–negative CRc rates were 46.7% (95% CI, 28.3%-65.7%) vs 36.7% (95% CI, 19.9%-56.1%). Additionally, the median duration of response (DOR) was 10.4 months (95% CI, 5.3-not evaluable [NE]) vs 5.6 months (95% CI, 3.8-6.8) in each arm. After a median follow-up of 10.0 months in the AK117 arm and 8.8 months in the placebo arm, the median overall survival (OS) was not reached (NR; 95% CI, 9.1-NE) vs 8.3 months (95% CI, 7.5-NE) in each arm (HR, 0.46; 95% CI, 0.20-1.06). The OS rates in each arm were 83.3% (95% CI, 64.5%-92.7%) vs 73.2% (95% CI, 53.4%-85.6%) at 6 months and 78.7% (95% CI, 58.2%-89.9%) vs 43.1% (95% CI, 22.0%-62.6%) at 9 months. The median event-free survival (EFS) was 9.1 months (95% CI, 5.8-11.4) vs 6.9 months (95% CI, 4.2-8.2) in each arm (HR, 0.46; 95% CI, 0.22-0.94), with respective 6-month EFS rates of 67.8% (95% CI, 45.3%-82.6%) vs 55.5% (95% CI, 32.5%-73.4%) and 9-month rates of 53.2% (95% CI, 31.1%-71.2%) vs 14.1% (95% CI, 2.4%-35.5%). “AK117 combined with [azacitidine/venetoclax] demonstrated clinically meaningful efficacy with improved response quality, durable remission, and favorable survival trends. All these efficiency benefits were achieved with a manageable and comparable safety profile,” study investigator Huafeng Wang, from The First Affiliated Hospital and Zhejiang University School of Medicine in Hangzhou, China, stated in the presentation. “This [study] supports AK117 as a promising addition to the [azacitidine/venetoclax] backbone, but it still needs further clinical development in this population.” AK117 was engineered as a next-generation, humanized IgG4 monoclonal antibody directed towards CD47. Both arms of the agent bind to CD47 on the same red blood cell (RBC) in a way that precludes RBC bridging and hemagglutination due to unique binding geometry, necessitating a priming dose to manage anemia. 2 In the phase 2 study, patients were randomly assigned 1:1 to receive AK117 plus azacitidine/venetoclax (n = 30) or placebo in combination with azacitidine/venetoclax (n = 30). Across each 4-week cycle, patients received AK117 or placebo at 20 mg/kg intravenously on day 1 every 2 weeks; venetoclax at 100 mg on day 1, 200 mg on day 2 of cycle, and 400 mg daily thereafter every 4 weeks; and azacitidine at 75 mg/m 2 subcutaneously on days 1 to 7 every 4 weeks. The trial’s primary end points were safety and CRc rate per ELN 2022 criteria. Secondary end points included ORR, MRD-negative CRc, duration of CRc, EFS, and OS. Patients 18 years and older with newly diagnosed AML ineligible for intensive chemotherapy, an ECOG performance status of 3 or lower or 2 or lower if 75 years or older, and adequate organ function were eligible for enrollment on the trial. The median patient age was 61.6 years (range, 23.7-74.4) in the AK117 arm and 64.0 years (range, 22.2-76.4) in the placebo arm; 50.0% vs 43.3% of patients in each arm were female. Most patients in each arm had an ECOG performance status of 2 (76.7% vs 83.3%), and favorable risk per ELN 2024 guidelines (50.0% vs 53.3%). All patients experienced treatment-emergent adverse effects (TEAEs) of any grade. TEAEs of grade 3 or higher occurred in 96.7% of the AK117 arm and 93.3% of the placebo arm, and 66.7% vs 66.7% in each group experienced serious AEs. Three patients in the experimental arm discontinued treatment due to TEAEs compared with 2 in the placebo arm. Additionally, fatal TEAEs were reported in 16.7% and 30.0% of each respective arm. The most common TEAEs in the AK117 and placebo arms, respectively, included decreased platelet counts (86.7% vs 66.7%), decreased white blood cell counts (83.3% vs 83.3%), decreased absolute neutrophil counts (80.0% vs 76.7%), and anemia (46.7% vs 50.0%). References Jin J, Zhang L, Zhang Q, et al. A phase II randomized study of AK117 versus placebo in combination with venetoclax and azacitidine in previously untreated acute myeloid leukemia patients ineligible for intensive chemotherapy. News | Articles | June 16, 2026 CD47 Antibody Combo Shows Meaningful Activity in Previously Untreated AML Author(s) Russ Conroy Fact checked by: Roman Fabbricatore Interact with this article Ask Scout! Ask questions, get summaries, explore topics. Instantly. Data from a phase 2 trial support further clinical development of AK117 in previously untreated acute myeloid leukemia. Advertisement “AK117 combined with [azacitidine/venetoclax] demonstrated clinically meaningful efficacy with improved response quality, durable remission, and favorable survival trends. All these efficiency benefits were achieved with a manageable and comparable safety profile,” according to study investigator Huafeng Wang. Combining the investigational anti-CD47 antibody AK117 with azacitidine (Vidaza) and venetoclax (Venclexta) demonstrated meaningful activity vs placebo plus azacitidine/venetoclax among patients with previously untreated acute myeloid leukemia (AML) who were ineligible for intensive chemotherapy, according to findings from a phase 2 trial (NCT06387420) presented at the European Hematology Association (EHA) 2026 Congress. 1 Data showed an overall response rate (ORR) of 80.0% (95% CI, 61.4%-92.3%) in the AK117 arm compared with 66.7% (95% CI, 47.2%-82.7%) in the placebo arm. The composite complete response (CRc) rates were 56.7% (95% CI, 37.4%-74.5%) vs 53.3% (95% CI, 34.3%-71.7%) in each respective arm, and the minimal residual disease (MRD)–negative CRc rates were 46.7% (95% CI, 28.3%-65.7%) vs 36.7% (95% CI, 19.9%-56.1%). Additionally, the median duration of response (DOR) was 10.4 months (95% CI, 5.3-not evaluable [NE]) vs 5.6 months (95% CI, 3.8-6.8) in each arm. After a median follow-up of 10.0 months in the AK117 arm and 8.8 months in the placebo arm, the median overall survival (OS) was not reached (NR; 95% CI, 9.1-NE) vs 8.3 months (95% CI, 7.5-NE) in each arm (HR, 0.46; 95% CI, 0.20-1.06). The OS rates in each arm were 83.3% (95% CI, 64.5%-92.7%) vs 73.2% (95% CI, 53.4%-85.6%) at 6 months and 78.7% (95% CI, 58.2%-89.9%) vs 43.1% (95% CI, 22.0%-62.6%) at 9 months. The median event-free survival (EFS) was 9.1 months (95% CI, 5.8-11.4) vs 6.9 months (95% CI, 4.2-8.2) in each arm (HR, 0.46; 95% CI, 0.22-0.94), with respective 6-month EFS rates of 67.8% (95% CI, 45.3%-82.6%) vs 55.5% (95% CI, 32.5%-73.4%) and 9-month rates of 53.2% (95% CI, 31.1%-71.2%) vs 14.1% (95% CI, 2.4%-35.5%). “AK117 combined with [azacitidine/venetoclax] demonstrated clinically meaningful efficacy with improved response quality, durable remission, and favorable survival trends. All these efficiency benefits were achieved with a manageable and comparable safety profile,” study investigator Huafeng Wang, from The First Affiliated Hospital and Zhejiang University School of Medicine in Hangzhou, China, stated in the presentation. “This [study] supports AK117 as a promising addition to the [azacitidine/venetoclax] backbone, but it still needs further clinical development in this population.” AK117 was engineered as a next-generation, humanized IgG4 monoclonal antibody directed towards CD47. Both arms of the agent bind to CD47 on the same red blood cell (RBC) in a way that precludes RBC bridging and hemagglutination due to unique binding geometry, necessitating a priming dose to manage anemia. 2 In the phase 2 study, patients were randomly assigned 1:1 to receive AK117 plus azacitidine/venetoclax (n = 30) or placebo in combination with azacitidine/venetoclax (n = 30). Across each 4-week cycle, patients received AK117 or placebo at 20 mg/kg intravenously on day 1 every 2 weeks; venetoclax at 100 mg on day 1, 200 mg on day 2 of cycle, and 400 mg daily thereafter every 4 weeks; and azacitidine at 75 mg/m 2 subcutaneously on days 1 to 7 every 4 weeks. The trial’s primary end points were safety and CRc rate per ELN 2022 criteria. Secondary end points included ORR, MRD-negative CRc, duration of CRc, EFS, and OS. Patients 18 years and older with newly diagnosed AML ineligible for intensive chemotherapy, an ECOG performance status of 3 or lower or 2 or lower if 75 years or older, and adequate organ function were eligible for enrollment on the trial. The median patient age was 61.6 years (range, 23.7-74.4) in the AK117 arm and 64.0 years (range, 22.2-76.4) in the placebo arm; 50.0% vs 43.3% of patients in each arm were female. Most patients in each arm had an ECOG performance status of 2 (76.7% vs 83.3%), and favorable risk per ELN 2024 guidelines (50.0% vs 53.3%). All patients experienced treatment-emergent adverse effects (TEAEs) of any grade. TEAEs of grade 3 or higher occurred in 96.7% of the AK117 arm and 93.3% of the placebo arm, and 66.7% vs 66.7% in each group experienced serious AEs. Three patients in the experimental arm discontinued treatment due to TEAEs compared with 2 in the placebo arm. Additionally, fatal TEAEs were reported in 16.7% and 30.0% of each respective arm. The most common TEAEs in the AK117 and placebo arms, respectively, included decreased platelet counts (86.7% vs 66.7%), decreased white blood cell counts (83.3% vs 83.3%), decreased absolute neutrophil counts (80.0% vs 76.7%), and anemia (46.7% vs 50.0%).
